inlineContent.text: Learn how to create a custom image pipeline to process and combine the incoming images into a final image.
Let’s start by creating a new view and naming it
. We also need to import
and
We will need an instance of
. Let’s create one by providing either a local or remove URL.
Let’s also create the
by providing the URL path to where the fonts are localted.
Now let’s add a
to view hierarchy and on appearance start the playback.
Next, we can add the
view as a video overlay and attach the player so the subtitles offset is updated.
Now let’s load the subtitles contents and load the track into the renderer.
In order to customise the image processing, we need to create a new type conforming to
, and inject this new pipeline into the renderer.
In order for us to be able to “merge” all the images into one single image, we we will need a helper method that will convert an
into a
and also will give us the rect where that image should be rendered in the canvas.
Let’s create another helper method that will take a list of
s and the bounding rect of the final image, and will combine all the images into one.
We can construct the rect for an
by using the provided width, height, x and y position from
Since the
from
is a single alpha channel monochrome bitmap plus a color, we need to convert this bitmap into a 4-channel RGBA bitmap.
The library provides a helper to convert a monochrome to a palettized bitmap, also to construct a
from a RGBA bitmap.
We will use the
to render all
s into a single
We will render this images at a 1x scale since
already takes care of scaling the canvas and the images based on the screen scale.
Let’s use the
method to render in the
and create a final
from the context.
Because the coordinate system when rendering in the
is different from the usual coordinate system (the origin starts at bottom left corner),
we will use a
to vertically flip the
so we can render from top left corner.
We need another helper method that will use the previous helper to flip the context, perform some actions, and then reset the context back to previous coordinate system.
In the rendering block, let’s iterate over all images and use the previous helper to render in the context from top left position.
While the
rendering will happen from top left cornet, the image rect y position still need to be adjusted by flipping it.
Also, since the image rect is in the canvas coordinate system, but we’re rendering in the final image bounding rect,
we need to convert the origin by substracting from canvas origin the bounding rect origin.
The last thing left to do is to draw the
into the
Finally, let’s run the app. And as expected, we can see the subtitles showing and being updated.
In this section we will create a basic view controller and a custom implementation of
that we will use to process the incoming images by creating
s and combining them using
